did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning, specifically a pre-trained Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architecture (DenseNet201) combined with a Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) classifier, to extract deep features and perform automated melanoma diagnosis (classification of dermoscopic images) on the PH2 benchmark dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents an original experimental study proposing an automated melanoma detection method using a pre-trained CNN (DenseNet201) and MLP classifier. It details a specific methodology (boundary localization, ROI extraction), describes the experimental setup on the PH2 dataset, and reports new quantitative results (accuracy, sensitivity, etc.) comparing the proposed approach with other classifiers and architectures.

title: Towards Exploring Deep Features for Efficient Melanoma Diagnosis in Dermoscopic Images
journal: 2022 OITS International Conference on Information Technology (OCIT)
brief_summary: This study proposes an automated method for melanoma diagnosis using dermoscopic images to address challenges like inter-class consistency and limited training data. The approach involves preprocessing images via boundary localization and cropping to extract regions of interest, followed by feature extraction using the pre-trained DenseNet201 CNN architecture. These deep features are classified using a Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) to distinguish between melanoma and benign lesions. The method was evaluated on the PH2 benchmark dataset, which contains 200 images split 70:30 for training and testing. Experimental results demonstrate that the DenseNet201 combined with MLP outperforms other CNN architectures and classifiers, achieving an accuracy of 96.66%, sensitivity of 83.33%, and specificity of 100%. The findings suggest this combination is superior to existing state-of-the-art techniques for efficient melanoma detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: Malignant skin cancer class; 40 instances in the PH2 dataset; grouped as 'melanoma' for binary classification against non-melanoma.
Benign skin lesion classes; 80 normal nevi and 80 atypical nevi in the PH2 dataset; merged to form the 'non-melanoma' group for binary classification.

all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification of dermoscopic images into 'melanoma' and 'non-melanoma' (benign) classes using a DenseNet201 CNN for feature extraction and an MLP classifier. The PH2 dataset was merged into two groups: non-melanoma (normal nevi and atypical nevi) and melanoma. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, and F1-score.

datasets_used.name: PH2 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public benchmark dataset containing dermoscopic images of skin lesions, used for evaluating the proposed melanoma diagnosis method.

use.training: Used for training the model; 70% of the dataset (140 images) was split for training.
use.testing: Used for testing the model; 30% of the dataset (60 images) was split for testing.
use.other: The normal nevi and atypical nevi classes were merged to form a 'non-melanoma' group for binary classification against melanoma.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, F1-score) without statistical significance testing.

PH2.dataset_description: 200 dermoscopic images (80 normal nevi, 80 atypical nevi, 40 melanoma). Merged into binary classes: non-melanoma (160 images) and melanoma (40 images). Split 70:30 for training (140 images) and testing (60 images).

Comparison of Classifiers with DenseNet2.description: Evaluation of DenseNet201 features with various classifiers on preprocessed images.
Comparison of Classifiers with DenseNet2.notes: DenseNet201+MLP achieved the best sensitivity, accuracy, and F1-score compared to other classifiers.

Comparison of CNN Architectures with MLP.description: Evaluation of different CNN architectures with MLP classifier on preprocessed images.
Comparison of CNN Architectures with MLP.notes: DenseNet201 obtained greater classification performance than other deep CNN networks. DenseNet201+MLP achieved an AUC of 97%.

Impact of Preprocessing (Original vs Pre.description: Comparison of DenseNet201+MLP performance on original vs. preprocessed (ROI extracted) images.
Impact of Preprocessing (Original vs Pre.notes: Accuracy, sensitivity, and F1-score of melanoma detection increased by ~10%, ~25%, and ~27.27% respectively when using preprocessed images compared to original dermoscopic images.

Comparison with Existing Techniques.description: Comparison of the proposed method with state-of-the-art methods from literature on the PH2 dataset.
Comparison with Existing Techniques.notes: The proposed method (DenseNet201+MLP) outperformed existing state-of-the-art approaches on the PH2 benchmark dataset.

architecture_used.input_size: 224x224 or 299x299 (depending on the specific CNN architecture used in comparisons; DenseNet201 typically uses 224x224)

architecture_details.feature_extraction_layer: Global average pooling layer (second last layer)

training_details.optimizer: Not explicitly reported (used Sklearn and Keras libraries)
training_details.hyperparameters: Tuned experimentally for the MLP classifier

preprocessing_techniques: Boundary localization to locate lesion borders
Region of Interest (ROI) extraction via cropping to excise unneeded tissue and retain vital lesion regions
Bitwise AND operation between original RGB images and binary masks
Cropping of all black rows and columns from the new image
Image resizing to handle varying dimensions resulting from ROI extraction
Pixel intensity normalization (mean and variance normalization) to ensure numerical stability and consistent feature value ranges
conclusions: The proposed method combining DenseNet201 for feature extraction and a Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) classifier outperformed other classifiers (SVM, RF, LR, AB, DT, NB, LDA, KNN) and deep CNN architectures (VGG16, VGG19, Inception V3, ResNet50, MobileNet) on the PH2 dataset.
The DenseNet201 + MLP model achieved an accuracy of 96.66%, sensitivity of 83.33%, specificity of 100%, precision of 100%, and an F1-score of 90.90%.

Preprocessing dermoscopic images via boundary localization and Region of Interest (ROI) extraction significantly improved performance compared to using original images, increasing accuracy by approximately 10%, sensitivity by 25%, and F1-score by 27.27%.
The proposed method achieved superior classification performance compared to existing state-of-the-art techniques reported in the literature on the PH2 benchmark dataset.

DenseNet201 demonstrated superiority over other well-known CNN architectures in identifying melanomas in skin lesions, attributed to its ability to facilitate feature reuse and improve feature flow with fewer parameters.

The study acknowledges that limited training data and inter-class consistency/intra-class variability in dermoscopic images pose challenges for automated melanoma diagnosis, which the proposed ROI-based deep feature extraction approach helps mitigate.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the PH2 benchmark dataset (200 images), which limits the generalizability of the results due to the small sample size and lack of external validation on diverse, multi-center datasets.

The dataset is highly imbalanced, with only 40 melanoma cases compared to 160 non-melanoma cases (merged normal and atypical nevi), which may bias the model towards the majority class despite the reported high specificity.

No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the superiority of the proposed method over state-of-the-art techniques; comparisons are based solely on descriptive metrics.
The paper does not report standard deviation or variance for the performance metrics, making it impossible to assess the stability or robustness of the results across different data splits or runs.

The preprocessing step involves boundary localization and ROI extraction using provided binary masks from the PH2 dataset; this reliance on ground-truth masks for preprocessing may introduce data leakage if the masks were not strictly held out during feature extraction, although the paper implies a standard split.

The model was evaluated only on the PH2 dataset; the authors acknowledge in the future work section that the method's effectiveness needs evaluation on 'vast and diverse skin lesion datasets' to confirm clinical relevance.
Hyperparameters for the MLP classifier were tuned experimentally without specifying the search space or optimization method, which limits reproducibility.

The reported specificity of 100% and precision of 100% for the DenseNet201+MLP model on the test set is unusually high for medical imaging tasks and may indicate overfitting to the small test set (60 images) or class imbalance effects.
